summary:
- **Core topic/purpose:** Petition/complaint by **Theodore Hubback** seeking redress over **allegedly defamatory remarks** about him in an official report by **J.F.F. (J.J.) Gregg** and requesting either **access to the report** and/or an **independent inquiry** where he could respond and cross-examine witnesses.  
- **Geography & setting:** **Pahang, Federated Malay States (F.M.S.)**, especially **Kuala Lipis** and **Ulu Tembeling**, connected to the **King George V National Park** (including the former **Gunong Tahan Game Reserve** area).  
- **Triggering event:** A **July 1939** inquiry/report by District Officer **Gregg** into local Malay claims to “rights/privileges” inside the Park (e.g., collecting forest produce, fishing, travel routes), under **Section 16(i) of the King George V National Park (Pahang) Enactment 1939**.  
- **Hubback’s allegations:** He argues Gregg relied on **ex parte statements** taken in his absence, made **incorrect factual/deductive claims**, and labeled Hubback’s actions as **illegal**, harming his reputation and (in Hubback’s view) threatening the Park’s “inviolability” as a wildlife sanctuary; Hubback notes he served as **Chief Game Warden** (unpaid) with statutory duties under the **Wild Animals and Birds Protection Enactment, 1925**.  
- **Administrative responses/decisions:** The **British Resident, Pahang (C.C. Brown)** and later the **High Commissioner** refused to provide Hubback a copy of Gregg’s report, treating it as a **confidential official document**, and Pahang stated it would take **no further action** (letter **14 May 1940**).  
- **Escalation to London & procedure dispute:** Hubback appealed to **Lord Lloyd** (Secretary of State for the Colonies) on **18 May 1940**, insisting he was **not a government servant** and should be able to petition directly; officials reiterated the rule that petitions must go **through the Governor/High Commissioner** and Hubback threatened to raise the matter in the **House of Commons** (letter **28 July 1940**).  
- **Outcome at Colonial Office:** After review, the **Secretary of State (Lord Moyne)** decided (draft/letter **6 May 1941**) there were **no grounds** to grant Hubback either an **independent inquiry** or a **copy of Gregg’s report**, emphasizing its **confidential/official-use** status; internal minutes characterize Hubback’s complaint as “somewhat extravagant,” though one note acknowledges the inquiry procedure (ex parte evidence) was “very unsatisfactory.”
